topic.what: First domain-specific jailbreak benchmark for evaluating LLM safety in hardware security workflows
topic.how: Proposes HarmChip with 16 domains, 120 threats, 360 prompts across Easy/Hard tiers using automated 3-stage pipeline with LLM-as-Judge evaluation
topic.why_matters: Hardware threats are irreversible once fabricated; existing safety mechanisms fail to distinguish legitimate security work from malicious manipulation when both use identical engineering terminology

characteristics.what_it_measures: Percentage of LLM responses providing substantive exploit-relevant content to adversarial hardware-security prompts
characteristics.context: Bimodal distribution; safety-hardened models (Step-3.5, Gemini-3.1P, GPT-5.4, Qwen-397B) maintain 0-17% ASR while open-weight models (Devstral, GPT-4.1, LLaMA-4M) reach 94-100%

characteristics.what_it_measures: Number of fine-grained hardware security domains covered
characteristics.vs_baseline: First benchmark to cover end-to-end hardware lifecycle
characteristics.evidence: Table I and Figure 1 threat taxonomy
characteristics.context: Spans supply chain, RTL design, synthesis, physical implementation, post-silicon, and emerging areas

characteristics.what_it_measures: Rate of over-refusal on legitimate security queries containing terms like 'Trojan insertion' or 'side-channel analysis'
characteristics.vs_baseline: Keyword-sensitive guardrails block red-teaming and defensive security work
characteristics.context: Illustrated via case studies: security auditors blocked while disguised attacks succeed

characteristics.what_it_measures: Rate of compliance with semantically disguised malicious hardware attacks
characteristics.value: High (12.50-100.00% ASR for most models on Hard benchmark)
characteristics.vs_baseline: General-purpose safety mechanisms fail to detect disguised threats
characteristics.context: Mid-tier models (Sonnet-4.6, Kimi-K2.5, Gemini-3F) show category-dependent ASR 22-83%

characteristics.what_it_measures: Total number of prompts and threat scenarios
characteristics.vs_baseline: Surpasses existing NLP/software benchmarks for hardware-specific evaluation
characteristics.context: Stratified into Easy (360) and Hard (360) variants; 960 prompts generated, 360 assigned per tier

characteristics.what_itasures: Number and diversity of LLMs evaluated
characteristics.vs_baseline: First comprehensive evaluation across proprietary and open-weight models
characteristics.context: Includes dense and MoE architectures, 196B to 1T parameters

good_for: identifying alignment paradox in security-critical domains
benchmarking model safety across hardware design workflows
comparing safety robustness of frontier LLMs
not_for: replacing formal verification of hardware designs
evaluating general NLP safety beyond hardware domain
assessing non-LLM AI systems for hardware tasks
requires: context injection constraints for prompt generation

novelty: First domain-specific jailbreak benchmark for hardware security covering 16 domains
Automated three-stage pipeline leveraging LLM-as-Attacker with difficulty stratification
Exposes alignment paradox: co-existence of false-negative and false-positive safety failures
Three-tier behavioral clustering: refusal-oriented, intermediate, high-compliance models
open_problems: Language-level evaluation may over/underestimate harm; functional maliciousness unverified
Advanced multi-turn adaptive jailbreak strategies not evaluated
Prompt coverage per threat scenario limited to 3 prompts per difficulty
Dynamic update mechanism for emerging threat vectors needed
Real EDA environment grounding (compile/simulate/synthesize) not explored
Connection between language-level failures and hardware-level impact not established
